sentences.text: Well, the, the train just kept on moving ahead of the Allied armies.
I've told you the food situation, and the escaping bit, and the Allied air attacks.
And it just went on like that, day after day.
Eventually, we got to Berlin.
Actually, we'd been moving ahead of the Allied forces, so we were traveling in an easterly direction through various towns.
And eventually we came, actually, to Berlin itself.
And we saw Berlin totally in ruins-- my birthplace, where I had started off from, originally.
Interesting feeling kind of thing to see it, in, in such a situation.
And that, that was roughly the 19th of April.
So it was about two weeks before the Russians got to Berlin, and Hitler killed himself, and all the other events that took place there.
So we were there right at the end, really.
And we went right through Berlin from one end to the other end.
And we saw many of the names that we recognized from the past, like Charlottenburg, especially, where I was born in hospital there.
And then, because the Russians were coming in from the eastern side and the British and Americans from the other side, the train turned south.
And then it just carried on and on.
I mean, we, we just couldn't understand why they were just keeping us, because obviously, it was all coming to an end.
But our idea is that, that there had been a directive issued from up high, at one time to Kramer in Belsen to say that these exchange people should be kept for potential exchange.
And this directive had never been countermanded, so Kramer had played along with it, and everybody else was playing along with it.
There would be nothing to stop, and, and they kept us.
They wanted to keep us, still, for exchange, although it was obviously ridiculous at that time.
The train went on and on and on.

And, and one night, we went to bed as usual-- and when I say bed, I mean we went to sleep in our-- we, we lay down on whatever we'd got left of our seats, because we'd used most of the wooden slats for cooking purposes.
But we lay down, maybe on the floor or something like that, like we did every other night.
And when we woke up in the morning, the SS guards had gone, and outside we could see Russian soldiers on horses.
And we'd been liberated.
It was just like that.
We didn't feel anything.
There, there was no celebrations and there was no singing and, and nothing, really.
No, we didn't feel anything.
Like I say, we were just dumbfounded.
And there it was.
We were free.
I don't think we had any idea what it was like to be free, because we, we had been in camps and under domination by the Germans for such a long time that we just didn't appreciate what was happening.
So basically, first of all, we had to explain to the Russians who we were, because they saw this trainload full of people, kind of thing, with stars, admittedly, and all that sort of thing.
And they wondered what the hell was going on.
And we couldn't speak Russian to try and communicate to them.
They couldn't speak Dutch, which is what we all spoke.
And we didn't want to speak German, because they were liable to just shoot Germans out of hand.
So it was quite difficult to explain to these Russian soldiers and some officers who we were.
You know, we'd been in the camps with our shaven heads or that we were Jews, that we were persecuted and all this sort of thing.
So eventually we managed to convince them that we were on their side, kind of thing.
And, and they said, you know-- let us go.
And, and we all put-- instead of the yellow stars, we all put big badges on-- red, white, and blue-- to show that we were from Holland, kind of thing.
And, and that was sort of the password as far as the Russians were concerned.
And they let us go, carry on.
They took no notice of us.
They carried on.
Because that was on the 23rd of April, 1945.
We'd been on the train for exactly two weeks, and we'd been liberated.
Two days later, the Russian army, on the 25th of April, met the American army at Torgau just down the road from where we were.
And, and there are lots of pictures in the papers about that-- the Russians meeting the Americans at the River Elbe, at Torgau.
And that was two days after we were liberated, very close to where we were.
But we went about our business in the normal way as we'd been used to on the, on the train.
And in the evening, the first evening of liberation, I came back to the train with my raw potatoes and my greens, you see.
And, and, and [NON-ENGLISH] and, and most of the other people, they had been to the nearby village of Trebitz, and they came back with white bread and honey and milk.
And they'd help themselves off the Germans, kind of thing, whatever they fancied.
So they looked at my raw potatoes and greens and, and thought I was mad and I was an idiot, kind of thing.
And I said I would do better next day.
And on the next day, then, I was very lucky, because I found the nearby cheese factory.
And I also found a wheelbarrow.
So in the evening, I came back with a wheelbarrow full of tubes of cheese.
They were making tubes of cheese there.
So I had a wheelbarrow full of tubes of cheese, and I thought I had done very well that day.
And when all the others came back from Trebitz, they'd been in the cellars of the German houses, and they came back with food-- fruit and preserves and gateaus.
And, and they'd-- off the Germans, they'd taken their watches, and they came back with radios and bicycles and some of them with cars.
And some of them had actually turfed the Germans out of their houses and taken over the German houses in Trebitz.
So me with my wheelbarrow of tubes of cheese-- everybody thought I was an idiot again, kind of thing.
And I obviously hadn't caught on to the new situation at all.
But [NON-ENGLISH] was well to the forefront, and he'd done very well.
He'd got a motorbike, a thing which he proudly brought back to the train.
But then everybody else started to move into the village of Trebitz and turfing the Germans out of their houses.
And I just wasn't up to it.
By that time, I was actually very ill.
I had-- my legs had swollen tremendously, and I, I couldn't walk very well.
And I, I've now found out it's an illness called edema, if that's pronounced correctly.

I never really understand that illness and why I should have got it, but-- And, and the strange thing is that I've seen many films, and I've read many books, and I've never, ever seen anybody else-- everybody's always skeletons.
I've never seen anybody who has got bloated and swollen legs like I had.
And I've never seen any books where people seem to have mentioned this edema.
But yeah, anyway, that's what I had.
And people say, you get that just before you die.
So maybe that's why you don't see many people like that.
And, and that is my excuse why I didn't do so good in, on the food and everything else in our first days of liberation.
I just wasn't really with it, really.
And I was in bad condition.
And on top of that, I actually got typhus then.
And, and so did my brother, [NON-ENGLISH].
We were both taken away by the Russian army.
We were taken to a Russian army hospital near Riesa-- quite a distance from Trebitz.
And there, [NON-ENGLISH] and me, we both had typhus.
And we had very high temperatures.
But we survived.
We had good treatment there.
And we spent two weeks in the hospital there, and we survived.
And we went back to Trebitz.
And by that time, all the people off the train were living in Trebitz, in the houses and everything.
And it was very difficult for us to fit in, really.
And we, we said, basically, we really wanted to go back to Holland.
There's no point in hanging on there.
By that time, the, the war had finished, and Germany had been divided up into Russian, American, British, French zones.
And we wanted to go back to Holland.
And, and the first step to get there was to get to the American zone.
The Russians laid down transport for us and quite a number of others who wanted to go back to Holland.
And we were taken to Leipzig, which was in the American zone.
No.
It was very interesting that we'd forgotten all about Eve.
We-- well, as I'll explain in a minute, kind of thing-- we, we'd had our own problems on the train, foraging with food.
And we did sort of in the back of our minds know that she was with the Birnbaums at the orphanage.
And, and we did know that she was on the train, and we presumed that they were looking after themselves.
And we hadn't really seen her at all.
And then, you know, the, the first couple days of liberation, we were busy.
And then we had typhus for two weeks in a Russian hospital, far away from Trebitz.
So we hadn't-- that's our excuse that we hadn't the opportunity to see her.
And when we came back to Trebitz, we-- I don't know what happened, whether we were blanked out, or whether we were still in bad condition, but we just forgot about Eve.
And this is very bad to say, but--
So we were taken to Leipzig, together with a lot of other people who wanted to go back to Holland, Belgium, France, et cetera, anything where we all had to go to the American zone.
And there, we had the first bit of sort of processing, really.
And, and the problem there was that we didn't have any documents.
The only document that we had was Eve's birth certificate, but we didn't have Eve.
So that wasn't a lot of good.
And we basically didn't have anything.
I mean, I might have had my [NON-ENGLISH] from Westerbork, but that didn't do a lot of good.
Didn't have birth certificates, or anything like that.
So it was very difficult.
Whereas, with the Russians, we couldn't speak the language.
So they never even tried to interrogate us or ask our name or anything like that.
But the Americans, we, we could speak the language, and they had people who could speak German and Dutch, and everything really.

Because by that time, which was a couple of weeks after the end of the, the war, there were Germans moving in every possible direction-- refugees, ex-army people, ex-SS people, and, and they were all pretending to be Jews from concentration camps, kind of thing.
And nobody knew who anybody was.
And, and the Americans had to sort out.
So when we said we were Jews from Holland, kind of thing, they said, you know, prove it, kind of thing.
We had nothing, except we could speak Dutch.
But it was very difficult for the Americans.
So they put us all into a camp, again-- in a [NON-ENGLISH].
I never know what the English word for that is, but it's like barracks, multi-story kind of things, in an army camp, while they tried to sort us out.
Well, we had loads of foods, and chewing gum, and chocolate, and all those sort of things-- probably very bad for us.
And, and we remember being in Leipzig for several days.
And we had rows with our fellow inmates, kind of thing.
[NON-ENGLISH] remembers that, in particular.
We threw all their stuff out of the window, kind of thing, and, and--
I don't know about what.
Maybe about chewing gum, or God knows what-- something.
[LAUGHS] But we remember, there was, in particular, three boys-- the [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH] or whatever they were called.
Three of them-- they'd lost their parents, and they finished up in Israel later.
They were big mates of ours, but we always had rows-- us two against them three.
And, and [NON-ENGLISH] remembers very well that one day, we were on the second floor or third floor or something, and we threw all their stuff out of the window one day.
The Americans got very angry at us about it.
But we stayed in Leipzig for several days while they try and sort it out who was who.
And then they said, yes, you can go back to Holland.
And they made up a transport of people to go back to Holland, Belgium, and France-- of camp survivors, kind of thing.
And it was when we went out of this camp, in Leipzig, on an open truck, another open truck came into the camp.
And on this other open truck was our sister, Eve.
And, and basically, as I said, we'd forgotten all about her, really.
It was very bad, considering she was the only reason why we survived.
If it hadn't been for her, we'd have been in Sobibor or Auschwitz.
So it was very bad of us to have forgotten about her.
But let me just say that we were busy trying to survive ourselves.
And, and we just forgot about her.
We didn't really know how or what we could have done, actually, to try and find her.
And this was just one of these miracles, then, that seemed to happen in war.
If we'd have been five minutes earlier, she'd have been five minutes later, we might never have seen Eve again.
I don't know whether she would eventually have made her way back to Holland and, and England, because she had, did have a birth certificate in her name.
And maybe she would've been found eventually somewhere, because she was in Leipzig, so she was in America, in American zone.
If she had stayed in the Russian zone-- But the Birnbaums had brought them all over to the Leipzig [INAUDIBLE].
So I think we would've met her again, but nevertheless, it was remiss of us to have forgotten about her.
And anyway-- but we saw her on this truck, and we stopped the other truck.
And we got to Eve to transfer to our truck, and she came with us on a train back to Holland.
Not really.
I suppose we were surprised [LAUGHS] and shocked and amazed, and, and, you know, forgotten all about her.
But it was probably [NON-ENGLISH] who had spotted her or noticed her, and, and we got her and said goodbye to the Birnbaums and all that sort of thing.
These are, again, people who went off to Israel afterwards, and, and because we forgot all about the Holocaust and all that, we, we never said thank you to the Birnbaums.
And now they're dead.
We, we-- somebody sent us their address a few years ago, but meanwhile they've died.
So there are lots of things that we should have done that we haven't done.
We took Eve with us on the train-- cattle truck, it was another great, big, long train of cattle trucks.
But of course, this was just after the end of the war, and there weren't many [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] passenger coaches and trains in Germany around anyway.
So perhaps we were lucky to get a train of cattle trucks.
But this time, all the doors were open, and we actually enjoyed ourselves on the train.
In fact, most of the time, we sat on top of the train on the roof, which was very interesting when we went through bridges and tunnels and all that sort of thing.
But life seemed very cheap, and we weren't worried, really.
We had survived so much that we just took all these risks.
I remember, we jumped from one coach, one cattle truck to the next cattle truck, from roof to roof, while the train was running.
Don't think I'll do that again, kind of thing.
But we were mad.
It was perhaps a bit like that, you know.
We, we were over the moon, I suppose is the expression these days, in a way, and-- I don't know.
And it was full of survivors, that train.
And it took some time to go through Germany, because the tracks were bombed in many places, and we went through bombed-out cities, and all that.
And eventually, we finished up in Holland, in a place that-- until a few years ago, nobody had ever heard of, when I first gave the talk-- Maastricht.
And then we said, what's Maastricht?
Now, of course, everybody knows what's Maastrict-- in the southern edge corner of Holland.
And that's where we arrived.
And all the people who wanted to go back to Holland were unloaded there, and the train went on to Belgium and France with other survivors who went home there.
And, and this is really where the interrogation and all that really started, when we met the, met the Dutch authorities and the Allied Expeditionary Force, as they were called-- AEF.
And, and we were medically examined and showered and cleaned and dusted and DDT sprayed and all these sort of things.
And we have our registration card ticked off with all these medical situations that we had to go through.
And then they wanted to know, of course, our names, where we were born.
And Eve was all right, but as soon as she came to me and [NON-ENGLISH] and they saw that we were born in Berlin, the Dutch authorities said that we were German.
And we claimed that we-- the Nazis had taken the German-- and this is another thing that I forgot to mention is that another thing the Nazis did was that Jews couldn't be Germans.
So all Jews who were born in Germany were stateless.
That was one of the rules that the Nazis brought in.
So we said we were stateless.
But the Dutch authorities said, all those Nazi rules don't count anymore, you know.
You had to wear a yellow star.
Well, we don't have to wear a yellow star anymore now.
So all these Nazi laws don't exist anymore.
So the fact that they made you stateless doesn't count.
As far as we're concerned, you're German.
And Germans go in camps.
So [NON-ENGLISH] and me, we were sent backward.
We had our card and everything, and on the card it said we were sent to a camp in Vaals, not far from Maastricht.
And we were put in a camp together with Germans, including German SS officers.
We were in the same barracks with German SS.
And it was not very nice kind of thing, because they could see who we were, and we could see who they were.
Well, you know, they looked like SS.
I mean, some of them might have had their uniforms on, and all that sort of thing.
And they all spoke German, and they clicked their heels.
And they were Germans, all right.

And whilst the Dutch authorities tried to figure out who we were-- and we gave them names of lots of people, lots of friends of our parents who would vouch for us-- in the meantime, Eve-- we'd contacted a lady called [NON-ENGLISH], Aunt [NON-ENGLISH], who lived below us in [NON-ENGLISH] in Amsterdam [INAUDIBLE] who always looked after Eve.
And this is another bit that I forgot about.
I must just go back to this bit in Amsterdam, then.
In Amsterdam, there was, of course, one other opportunity-- apart from being deported-- and that was to go into hiding.
And, and we had the opportunity to go into hiding in, in Holland.
We had many Dutch friends.
My parents had very good friends, and many of them offered to hide us, either in Amsterdam or, or elsewhere in the countryside in Holland.
But going into hiding was extremely risky, not only for the Jews who went into hiding, but also for the Dutch citizens who, who were doing the hiding.
They could be sent to the Dutch concentration camps or could be shot, et cetera, or-- It was difficult.
So my father, again, never wanted to do it.
He, being-- sticking to the rules, et cetera.
He, he wouldn't do it.
My mother, I think, would've gone for the going into hiding, like the Franks did.
And we were in exactly the same opportunities.
But we didn't do it.
And it was-- even on that day when the roundup was there, when we were all caught and cordoned off, that morning, still, this [NON-ENGLISH] below us-- she was a widow on her own.
Particularly, she liked Eve.
She offered to hide us in her house there that day, but my parents wouldn't do it.
Anyway, she was always very good.
So when we got back to Maastricht, we, we contacted this [NON-ENGLISH].
And she came to Maastricht, and she collected Eve.
And Eve went with her to Amsterdam and stayed there.
We went to the camp.
And after a few days, we were let out of the camp, when they sorted out that we were all right.
And we were sent to a Jewish orphanage in Laren, not far from Naarden, where we had lived at one time.
And, and it was a Jewish orphanage with lots of other survivors and people and children who'd been in hiding and children in general of the Holocaust.
Meanwhile, Eve was in Amsterdam.
And in Laren, we started to go back to school again.
In fact, I went to the same school that I had started in Bussum.
After Naarden, I'd started another school in Bussum, a higher-- lyceum, a higher education school, and I went back to that same school in Bussum in 1945.
In the meantime, we contacted all the-- my parents' friends, and we got all our things back.
And in particular, we contacted our aunt in Palestine and the uncle in London.
And the uncle in London, particularly, he had found out about us.
They had been searching the lists, and all that sort of thing, and they heard we'd survived.
And in August he found out-- we came to Holland in June, July-- in August he found out where we were.
And he was in the British army.
And he came over to visit us in Holland in September, and he offered to take us back, or get us back into England.
And we said yes, you know, we, we wanted to do that.
So Eve-- he took Eve back to England straight away, because she had her British birth certificate.
She had no problems.
But for [NON-ENGLISH] and me, we couldn't go to England without a visa.
So we stayed on in Laren for some further time, in fact, for several months.
And in the meantime, my uncle and aunt got these entry visas for us.
And on the 23rd of November, 1945-- seven months exactly after we'd been liberated in Trebitz, we got to Harridge and on the boat train to Liverpool Street Station.
And there my uncle and Eve met us.
And in some ways, that's really the end of the story, unless we're going to carry on after the war.
You want to carry on?
I want to carry on.
What was it like being in England again?
Very nice, I would say, I would say.
Yes.
You know, it was really, in many ways, much better than being in Holland.
Because in Holland, we were in an orphanage.
And although we got on with the other kids and went to school and all that sort of thing, we could feel, now, that we hadn't got our parents, and we couldn't really figure out our life.
It was very nice to have family around us again.
I found out only yesterday, I think, by reading some of these letters, that my aunt in Israel also offered to take us in, in Palestine, at that time.
But I don't think we ever actually really knew it, or we decided on England, really.
We, we liked England the first time, and we always believed England was the right place for us.
And we always heard good things about England.
We liked the looks of England.
No, not really.
We-- my uncle and aunt lived in northwest Dollis Hill, in a flat, block of flats, 60 flats.
And it wasn't very big.
And [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], they had two children of their own by that time-- Peter and Ruth, usually referred to as [NON-ENGLISH] sustained from [NON-ENGLISH].
So with an extra three children, the flat wasn't nearly big enough, kind of thing, so they had great difficulties.
Nevertheless, they coped with us for the best part of a year, at first.

And I had expressed an interest in becoming an engineer, so they found work for me as an apprentice at a small tool-making factory, [INAUDIBLE] Precision Tool Making, in-- off Baker Street somewhere in London.
I went there every day.
And [NON-ENGLISH] went to school, and I think Eve may also have gone to school, or, or soon went to school.
We lived there for a year, and we met lots of [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] friends.
But generally, they couldn't really cope with us.
Not only was there too many people in the flat, but financially they were not well off.
And eventually, the idea was that I should go somewhere else.
And the Jewish Refugees Committee in London made contact.
Because I wanted to be an engineer, they thought the Midlands and Birmingham was the right place for me.
And the Jewish Refugees Committee arranged for me to come to Birmingham.
And there I met a lady who was called Ruth [INAUDIBLE] at the time, later Ruth [INAUDIBLE], and she was a fantastic lady in the refugee movement, really, who really looked after me ever so well.
Unfortunately, she's passed away now.
But she set me up in a hostel in Handsworth in Birmingham, where there were other refugees.
It was run by a lady, Mrs.
Miller.
These were all people who came just before the war, really-- Mrs.
Miller from Vienna and several of the boys who came there before the war.
And during the day, this Ruth [INAUDIBLE] arranged for me to get an apprenticeship at BSA Tools, a five-year engineering apprenticeship.
And I went there for five years every day on my bike.
And in the evenings, I studied, first of all for university entrance for my matriculation, as they called it in those days.
And the biggest problem I had with the matriculation-- I knew all the math and the science and all that sort of thing which you want to know.
The biggest problem was the English, because I couldn't really speak English sufficiently well enough to write the answers and, and all that sort of thing.
So I had to do a lot to of catching up on the English.
But I got my matriculation, and then there was an intermediate examination for bachelor of science.
It was different from what it is now.
And eventually, I embarked on an external London University degree course.
I don't think they have those sort of things nowadays.
It's open university now.
But it meant studying in evening classes in Birmingham for something like five years to get this degree from London University in mechanical engineering.
So I did this.
I worked during the day at BSA Tools.
I studied in the evening.
I went to technical college four or five days a week to study all the various subjects for this degree.
And, and I was much occupied by that.
And, and certainly, the Holocaust-- I forgot all about the Holocaust.
I never really thought about the Holocaust at all.
I really had-- really very busy.
I can't recollect that, really, no.
I don't think so.
Maybe they asked a few questions of what happened, and that sort of thing.
But we never gave them the full story.
I don't think they ever knew about the train and all those sort of things.
I regret now that we never discussed all these sort of things, because they probably could've filled me in on lots of things.
One, my father didn't come to England.
I know that.
I'm sure they knew all that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can only say that I-- first of all, I lived in Birmingham.
And, and when I first came over, we were, perhaps, reticent to talk to other people about our experiences.
Because nobody was interested in those days-- I mean, when Belsen was liberated, it was high profile, so I gather, having seen all the papers and speeches and documentation and all that.
But very soon afterwards, the mood, apparently, in Britain changed completely, in that Britain wanted to be friends with the Germans against the Russians.
And they didn't want to play up the atrocities in the concentration camps.
This is the story I've heard.
But one way or the other, nobody-- including my uncle and aunt-- nobody asked, really, about what happened and the story.
Nobody wanted to know.
And I wasn't particularly worried about it.
I didn't particularly want to tell anybody.
I didn't think it was very interesting, really.
